coredata.dc:title: Functional carbon dots from a mild oxidation of coal liquefaction residue
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Fuwei
Li, Qiqi
Tang, Tingting
Zhu, Jiayao
Gan, Xuemeng
Chen, Yaoyao
Li, Yizhao
Zhang, Su
Huang, Xueli
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: Effective utilization of the solid-waste coal liquefaction residue (CLR) is important for the development of direct coal liquefaction technology, but remains big challenge due to the high ash of about 30 wt% in CLR. Herein, we propose a mild oxidation method to prepare functional carbon dots (CDs) using CLR as the carbon precursor and piranha solution (H2O2/H2SO4) as the oxidant. Through catalytic hydrogenation, the highly conjugated structure of coal converts to weakly linked structure by aliphatic chains in CLR, leading to an improved oxidation reactivity. The highly reactive aliphatic chains are oxidized and cut during oxidation, leading to the formation of CDs with enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites and simultaneous removal of ash. To present the special properties of CDs, we prepare a microporous carbon with high-surface area and compaction density (1345 m2 g−1 and 0.86 g cm−3) through homogeneous activation of CDs, performing both high gravimetric and volumetric capacitance for supercapacitors. CDs are also used as the functional reinforcing phase for activated carbon nanofibers, enabling high surface area, improved mechanical property, good conductivity, and extremely high rate performance for supercapacitors. Our work provides a new avenue for high efficient and high value-added utilization of CLR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal liquefaction residue
Chemical oxidation
Carbon dots
Supercapacitors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CD-reinforced carbon nanofiber fabric (RCNF)
Surface area and elemental analysis of microporous carbon
Comprehensive performance analysis of CNF, RCNF, and activated RCNF

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046, 21766036, 52100166), the Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Xinjiang, the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of CDs through mild oxidation.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XRD patterns, (c) Raman spectra, (d) C1s spectra, (e) XPS survey spectra, and (f) TGA curves in N2 Atmosphere of the CDs and CLR.
(a) SEM, (b) HRTEM images, (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm, (d) pore size distribution, (e) XPS survey, and (f) C1s spectrum of the as-prepared microporous carbon.

Electrochemical performances of the microporous carbon in three-electrode system: (a) CV curves at carious scan rates, (b) GCD curves at different current densities, (c) Nyquist plot and (d) gravimetric and volumetric capacitances. (e) Comparison of the gravimetric and volumetric capacitance of microporous carbon with other carbon materials. (f) Cycle performance of the microporous carbon at 10 A g−1.

Photographs of (a, b) the as-prepared RCNF fabrics after multiple rolling, (c) a 50 g weight pulled up by 0.6 mg RCNF fabrics (4*23 cm). SEM images of (d) CNF and (e) RCNF. (f) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of CNF, RCNF, and activated RCNF. (g) The electric conductivity and tensile stress of the as-prepared samples.

Electrochemical performance of activated RCNF in measured by the three-electrode system: (a) CV curves at various scan rates, (b) GCD curves at different current densities, (c) rate performance, and (d) bode plot.

head.ce:title: Functional carbon dots from a mild oxidation of coal liquefaction residue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors with equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functional carbon dots with conjugated core and enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups are prepared in large-scale through mild oxidation of the solid-waste coal liquefaction residue, promoting the efficient and high-value-added utilization of coal resource.

Effective utilization of the solid-waste coal liquefaction residue (CLR) is important for the development of direct coal liquefaction technology, but remains big challenge due to the high ash of about 30 wt% in CLR. Herein, we propose a mild oxidation method to prepare functional carbon dots (CDs) using CLR as the carbon precursor and piranha solution (H2O2/H2SO4) as the oxidant. Through catalytic hydrogenation, the highly conjugated structure of coal converts to weakly linked structure by aliphatic chains in CLR, leading to an improved oxidation reactivity. The highly reactive aliphatic chains are oxidized and cut during oxidation, leading to the formation of CDs with enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites and simultaneous removal of ash. To present the special properties of CDs, we prepare a microporous carbon with high-surface area and compaction density (1345 m2 g−1 and 0.86 g cm−3) through homogeneous activation of CDs, performing both high gravimetric and volumetric capacitance for supercapacitors. CDs are also used as the functional reinforcing phase for activated carbon nanofibers, enabling high surface area, improved mechanical property, good conductivity, and extremely high rate performance for supercapacitors. Our work provides a new avenue for high efficient and high value-added utilization of CLR.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct coal liquefaction is the technology of catalytic converting coal to clean liquid fuel through hydrocracking the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in coal to small aliphatic molecules[1]. It can effectively reduce the emission of industrial waste and toxic gases as well as improving the coal utilization, thereby, showing great potential for sustainable development of coal industry. However, except for producing clean liquid fuel, direct coal liquefaction inevitably generates coal liquefaction residues (CLR) of about 30 wt% of the feed coal[2,3]. CLR contains not only high ash of ca. 35 wt% mainly coming from the raw coal and the added catalysts[4], but also considerable amount of valuable carbon components including asphaltenes, preasphaltene, and heavy oil[2,5,6]. It would be meaningful if we can use valuable organic components to prepare high-value carbon materials rather than the traditional ways of paving road, burning, or gasification[7], which can increase the economic value and decrease environmental pollution.

Currently, solvent extraction is one of the most commonly used ways to extract useful hydrocarbon components from CLR based on the rule “like dissolves like”[8–10]. The hydrocarbons can be used to prepare highly conductive carbon materials due to its enrich in aromatic ring structure[11,12]. For example, asphaltene extracted from CLR as carbon source was used to prepare porous carbon nanosheet by an in-situ sheet-structure-directing agent from urea thermal polymerization. The porous carbon nanosheets with a graphitized-like ribbon structure were further obtained after KOH activation. As the supercapacitor electrode material, the sample showed a specific capacitance of 282.9 F g−1 even at 100 A g−1 [13]. Asphaltene extracted from CLR as high-molecular polymer was also used to prepare carbon nanofibers fabrics through electrospinning, HNO3 preoxidization, air stabilization, and carbonization processes. The obtained sample exhibited good rate capability (143 F g−1 at 100 A g−1) and long lifespan (98% of its initial capacitance after 10,000 cycles) as supercapacitor electrode[14]. The research works indicate that the heavy organic matters in CLR are in favor of preparing high value-added carbon materials, which may further improve the economic efficiency of direct coal liquefaction technology[12,15]. But before that, it is necessary to remove the ashes from CLR by a simple and clean way.

Carbon dots (CDs)[16] are newly rising zero-dimensional carbon materials, which have been widely used in optics, catalysis, biomaterials, and energy storage due to the novel quantum size effect, semiconductor property, and edge effects[17,18]. Traditional preparation of CDs is mainly divided into top-down methods (eg. arc discharge, laser ablation, and chemical oxidation)[17,19,20] and bottom-up methods (eg. hydrothermal polymerization, microwave irradiation, and pyrolysis)[21–23]. At present, bottom-up methods have achieved kilogram-scale synthesis[24]. However, the obtained CDs usually show a completely amorphous structure, which is not suitable to prepare highly conductive carbon materials. By contrast, CDs from the top-down approach using conjugated precursors are favorable to prepare highly conductive functional carbon materials, but the yield is still low (below gram-scale) because the typical carbon source such as activated carbon, carbon black, and coal possess strong chemical inertness[25] Moreover, strong oxidation systems such as HNO3/H2SO4 and KMnO4/H2SO4 are commonly applied to cut the inert precursors, resulting in severe safety risk, environmental pollution, and release of toxic gases (eg. NO2, SO2)[26,27]. Therefore, developing simple and effective method for large-scale preparation of CDs is highly demanded.

Herein, we report a mild oxidation strategy to prepare CDs in large-scale using CLR as the precursor, and piranha solution (H2O2/H2SO4) as the oxidant. Through catalytic hydrogenation, CLR shows higher chemical reactivity than raw coal because the highly conjugated aromatic structure of coal is partially cut to small-sized conjugated molecules weakly linked by aliphatic chains[28]. The CDs are prepared by cutting the reactive aliphatic chains through mild oxidation and stepwise separation. The obtained CDs contains un-detectable ash after separation and purification. To demonstrate the novel properties of the as-prepared CDs, we have prepared microporous carbon with high surface area and high density through homogeneous activation of CDs. We also used CDs as the reinforcing phase for improving the mechanical property and conductivity of carbon nanofibers. The microporous carbon and activated carbon nanofiber fabric show outstanding electrochemical performance as the electrode materials for supercapacitors. Our work provides a new and simple approach for effective utilization of CLR to prepare high value-added carbon materials.

All the chemical reagents were used as received without any purification. CDs were prepared through a mild oxidation using CLR (Shenhua Coal to Liquid and Chemical Co., Ltd.) as the precursor. As schemed in Fig. 1 . The piranha solution was first prepared by adding 30 mL H2O2 solution (30 wt%) to 70 mL concentrated H2SO4 (98 wt%) dropwise at 0 °C with mild stirring (the preparation should be done under protection due to the oxidation and corrosion risk). 5 g CLR was put into 500 mL beaker. After careful adding 100 mL piranha solution, the beaker was heated at 50 °C for 2 h by water bath. The reason for choosing 2 h as the optimized oxidation time was presented in Figure S1. Subsequently, 100 mL of the deionized water was carefully added into the beaker to stop the reaction. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to about 12 by using NaOH solution (1 mol L-1). The CDs are collected as the precipitate after centrifugation (4000 rpm, 5 min), washed to neutral by dialysis, and dried at 80 °C in an oven.

0.5 g CDs and 0.25 g KOH were thoroughly dispersed into 35 mL ethanol through vigorous sonication and stirring. After being dried at 80 °C, the fine powder was compressed in a commercially available pressure mold (inner diameter of 13 mm) under 10 MPa using a laboratory hydraulic machine. The obtained thin tablets were carbonized at 800 °C for 1 h under N2 atmosphere with a hearing rate of 2 °C min−1. The microporous carbon was obtained through washing, purification, and drying according to our previous work[29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD-reinforced carbon nanofiber fabric (RCNF)
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of RCNF, 0.4 g CDs and 0.4 g polyacrylonitrile powder (PAN, J&K Scientific Co. Ltd., average molecular weight: 150,000 Da) were completely dispersed into 5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (AR, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute) to form a homogeneous spinning solution. The electrospinning was carried out using an applied voltage of 18 kV. The temperature and humidity were controlled to be 35 °C and 35%, respectively. The fabric collected after electrospinning was pre-oxidized at 280 °C in air for 2 h, subsequently carbonized at 800 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere.

For preparation of the activated RCNF, the pre-oxidized sample was carbonized at 500 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere. Subsequently, activated RCNF is prepared by carbonizing a mixture of the 500 °C carbonized RCNF and KOH with a mass ratio of 1:3 at 800 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere. The detail is given in our previous work[30].

The structure and morphology of the materials were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F, Japan), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA, Germany), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker VERTEX 70, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi). The volumetric adsorption apparatus (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics, USA) was used to measure the specific surface area and pore size distribution. The mechanical property of the carbon nanofiber fabrics was measured by a static mechanical device (HSKT, Tinius Olsen, USA)[27,28].The thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was measured on a NETZSCH STA 449 F3 simultaneous thermal analyzer in an N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

The electrodes were prepared by coating the samples on nickel foam as follow: the microporous carbon, conductive carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene with the mass ratio of 85: 10: 5 were uniformly mixed in ethanol. Then, the slurry was coated on nickel foam (1 × 1 cm2) by blade coating and vacuum dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The mass loading of the active material in one electrode is ca. 2 mg cm−2. The activated RCNF, were cut into square pieces (1.0 × 1.0 cm2, ∼1.0 mg) and pressed between two nickel foams to make working electrodes. The electrodes were tested by three-electrode systems in KOH aqueous electrolyte (6 mol L-1) according to our previous works[31]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) measurements were measured using CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed with a frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The potential window was set between −0.9 and 0.1 V.

The compositions of CLR and the contained ash compositions are listed in Table S1 and S2. Catalytic hydrogenation during direct coal liquefaction breaks the large-area conjugated structure of coal to small aromatic domains weakly linked by aliphatic chains (Fig. 1), leading to a high chemical reactivity[11,32]. The piranha solution can selectively oxidize and cut highly reactive aliphatic chains to CO2 and H2O, leaving behind the stable conjugated debris, thus leading to the formation of CDs with enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites[33,34]. CDs preparation contains multiple separation and purification steps. Firstly, NaOH solution not only used as neutralizer to reduce the pollution of wastewater, but also provided an alkaline environment to well disperse CDs due to the enriched acidic functional groups. Almost all of the ashes and unreacted CLR were separated by centrifugation process. Secondly, HCl solution was added into the mixed solution to precipitate CDs, leading to small soluble molecules in the supernatant. Finally, the small molecules with the molecular weight<3,500 Da were separated by dialysis. The yield of CDs calculated from the mass ratio of the obtained CDs to the dosage of CLR is ca. 36% (Figure S2), almost one magnitude higher than the previous works[34]. CDs show significantly improved dispersibility in water than CLR, as from the photographs of brown CDs suspension compared with the un-dispersible CLR (Fig. 1). Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs exhibit green fluorescence property when excited by 365 nm ultraviolet light (Figure S3). HRTEM images indicate that CDs have an average diameter of 6.8 nm (Fig. 2 a). The nanosheet (marked by white circle in Fig. 2b) shows a highly disordered structure due to the low crystallinity of CLR after hydrogenation.

In the FT-IR spectrum of CLR (Fig. 3 a), the strong peaks located at 3000–2700 cm−1 and 1489 cm−1 are the aliphatic C–H vibration from –CH-, –CH2-, and –CH3 groups, indicating the formation of aliphatic chains by catalytic hydrogenation. The high intensity peak located at ca. 1600 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of aromatic structure (C = C) from the remained polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon region[35]. The aliphatic chains in CLR are of high reactivity during oxidation, which can be easily cut by hydroxyl free radicals (Fig. 1)[36]. Therefore, through treating by piranha solution for only two hours, the obtained CDs show a broad peak ranging from 3670 to 1850 cm−1 and a shoulder peak at around 1450 cm−1, demonstrating the breaking of aliphatic chain by oxidation and formation of oxygen-bearing functional groups such as –COOH and -C = O. It should be noticed that the 1600 cm−1 peak is still remained in the spectrum of CDs, revealing their polycyclic aromatic core structure.

Next, XRD patterns are presented to determine structure of CDs (Fig. 3b). The diffraction peaks located at 24° and 43° correspond to the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of carbon, respectively[37–39]. Their broad and dispersive shape demonstrate the low crystallinity of CLR and CDs. It is interesting that the (002) peak of CDs shifts to a higher degree compared with that of CLR, indicating a decreased interlayer spacing. It is well-known that when graphite is oxidized to prepare graphite oxide, the decoration of oxygen-bearing functional groups on the basal plain of graphite leads to the expanded interlayer spacing, as presented by the down-shift of (002) peak from 26° to ca. 10°. The “abnormal” up-shifted (002) peak of the oxygen-enriched CDs in this work indicates that the oxygen-bearing functional groups are not located on the basal plain of CDs. Moreover, destructive functional groups such as –COOH, –COOC-, and -C = O are mainly decorated at the edge sites of graphene platelets because of the atomic activity and the steric hindrance effect[39]. Therefore, by mild oxidation and cutting of the aliphatic chains of CLR, the oxygen-bearing functional groups of CDs are mainly located at the edge sites of CDs.

Mild oxidation can maintain the polycyclic aromatic cores of the as-prepared CDs. As shown in the Raman spectra (Fig. 3c), the fitted as four Gaussian peaks located at 1210, 1360, 1510, and 1600 cm−1 are corresponding to the T, D, D’ and G-band, respectively[40]. In the Raman spectrum of CDs, the G band is shifted to left and the D band becomes prominent, indicating the sp3 carbon by functionalization[41,42]. But unlike those CDs prepared through other methods[43,44], the G band of as-prepared CDs shows a higher intensity than the D band even through a strong oxidation. The D to G peak area ratio of CDs is only 0.88 lower than that of CLR (0.96), indicating that the highly conjugated core structure of CDs is well-kept.

Mild oxidation and subsequent purification can also remove ash and impurities in CLR, leading to high purity of the obtained CDs. As seen from the XRD patterns, apart from the diffraction peaks of carbon, CLR shows many diffraction peaks mainly coming from the ash components such as Fe2O3, Fe0.96S, and SiO2 [45]. By contrast, CDs only show two characteristic carbon peaks. Moreover, the XPS survey spectrum of CLR (Fig. 3d) shows that, apart from 90.8 at.% carbon and 5.4 at.% oxygen contents, it also contains 3.8 at.% other components such as Fe, Ca, and S due to the high ash and impurity contents. But CDs show a carbon content of 75 at.% and an increased oxygen content of 25 at.%. No other components are observed. The high purity of CDs is favorable for their practical and high-value-added application.

Fig. 3e shows the XPS C1s spectra of CLR and CDs. CLR only shows two peaks at 284.5 and 285.9 eV, corresponding to C = C in aromatic rings and small amount of C-O groups, respectively. The spectrum of CDs is clearly divided into C = C/C-O, C-O, -C = O, and –COOH components located at ca. 284.8, 285.9, 286.8, and 289.0 eV, respectively. The intensity of the peak assigned to –COOH group is significantly increased, which is in consistent with the FT-IR result.

The TG in N2 atmosphere was carried out to investigate the pyrolysis behavior of CLR and CDs (Fig. 3f). Pyrolysis of the CLR involves three stages. Before 350 °C, the release of adsorbed water and small-molecule gases leads to a slight weight loss of 8 wt%[46]. An obvious weight loss of 34 wt% is observed from 350 to 550 °C, which is ascribed to the devolatilization, such as dealkylation of aliphatic chains and dehydrogenation of naphthenic rings[47,48]. Thereafter, a stable plateau with a small weight loss of 1 wt% is ascribed to the gradual formation of stable carbon scaffold through polycondensation of the heavy aromatic components[6,49]. By contrast, CDs show a continuous weight loss during pyrolysis because of the gradual decomposition of various oxygen-bearing functional groups, which is quite different from CLR. For example, the mass loss before 300 °C is attributed to pyrolysis of the labile oxygen-bearing functional groups (such as COOH), yielding CO, CO2, and H2O[50,51]. In this temperature range, the weight loss of CDs is significantly larger than CLR, suggesting a large amount of –COOH content in CDs. Thereafter, the gradual weight loss is mainly due to the removal of stable oxygen-bearing functional groups such as -C = O, –COOC-, and phenolic hydroxyl group. CDs shows a 47% weight residual at the final testing temperature of 800 °C, lower than 57% of CLR because of their high oxygen content.

The above results show that CDs have conjugated cores with large number of oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites. This structure not only enable good hydrophilicity and reactivity, but also can be easily converted to large-area conjugate structure through high-temperature treatment, leading to good conductivity of the prepared carbon materials. Herein, we presented two examples to show the novel functions of CDs.

Because of high power density, rapid charge and discharge capabilities, and long cycle life, supercapacitors have been widely used in vehicles, electronic devices and power backups. Improving the volumetric capacitance of carbon electrode materials has become a key issue for meeting the increasing need for device miniaturization and lightweight[52,53]. However, due to the contradiction of the large surface area, abundant pores, and high density of carbon materials, it is still challenging for preparing carbon materials with both high surface area and high density of supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface area and elemental analysis of microporous carbon
ce:section.ce:para: To solve the above-mentioned problem, herein, we prepared a microporous carbon using CDs as precursors through homogeneous activation[29]. The microporous carbon is prepared by mixing CDs with a small amount of KOH and then thermally annealed in N2 atmosphere after compression using a laboratory hydraulic machine. The microscopic images demonstrate the carbon with a block morphology (Fig. 4 a) and an amorphous structure (Fig. 4b). It shows a type I N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm with a rapid adsorption in the low pressure region (P/P0 < 0.1) and a small hysteresis loop in the middle pressure region (0.5 < P/P0 < 0.9), indicating the existence of abundant micropores and a small amount of mesopores (Fig. 4c). It is worth noting that the microporous carbon shows a narrow pore size distribution mainly centered at 0.64, 0.80, and 1.15 nm (Fig. 4d). The dense block structure with a relatively low total pore volume of 0.59 cm3 g−1 enables a high surface area of 1345 m2 g−1 together with a high packing density of 0.86 g cm−3.

The surface chemical composition of the microporous carbon is investigated by XPS. The oxygen content deceases from 24 at.% for CDs to 11 at.% for microporous carbon after high temperature treatment (Fig. 4e). The XPS C1s spectrum (Fig. 4f) can is divided into three peaks located at around 284.8, 286.1, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to the C = C, C-O, and C = O components, respectively. Compared with CDs, the microporous carbon shows a decreased C = O content while a considerably increased C-O content, which is in accordance with the FT-IR analysis (Figure S4). These oxygen-bearing functional groups mainly play roles in providing pseudocapacitance and improving the surface wettability for electrode materials[54–56].

The CV curves of microporous carbon at different scan rates from 10 to 100 mV s−1 show a slightly distorted rectangle shape (Fig. 5 a). The GCD at different current densities from 1 to 100 A g−1 are also slightly distorted from the isosceles triangle (Fig. 5b), indicating energy storage mechanism is the combination of electrical double-layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance[57,58]. The porous structure with large specific surface area mainly contributed to the electric double-layer capacitance. Additional pseudocapacitance is provided by the oxygen-bearing function groups. EIS was used to investigate the electrochemical kinetics of the microporous carbon (Fig. 5c). The curve intersection toward the X-axis is almost overlapped with the zero point, indicating the high electrical conductivity. It shows a small semicircle diameter in the high frequency region and vertical tail in the low frequency, indicating robust charge-transfer and ion transport kinetics. The reason is ascribed to that homogeneous activation of CDs leads to the formation of highly conductive networks and interconnected porous structure, as proved in our previous work[29]. Moreover, relative high oxygen content enables good surface wettability towards KOH aqueous electrolyte, which is also beneficial for fast ion transport. It is worth mentioning that the microporous carbon exhibits high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of 262 F g−1/220 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and 103 F g−1/83 F cm−3 at 100 A g−1 (Fig. 5d), which are superior to most of the previously reported porous carbons (Fig. 5e)[29,59–66]. Moreover, it shows good cycle stability with no obvious capacitance fading after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5f). As a result, CDs originated from CLR are beneficial for constructing porous and conductive carbon because of their small sized structure, enriched functional groups, and highly conjugated core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comprehensive performance analysis of CNF, RCNF, and activated RCNF
ce:section.ce:para: Electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics (CNFs) have attracted considerable attention due to their unique nanofiber morphology, good chemical stability, and flexibility. It shows great potential in some aspects such as smart membranes, wearables electronic devices, and energy storage materials[67–69]. However, CNFs usually show poor mechanical strength and flexibility due to the inherent pores and cracks inside the nanofibers. They can easily break into small pieces by gentle pressing (Figure S5). Therefore, it is still challenging for preparing CNFs with large surface area, high strength, and high flexibility due to the contradiction between a strong carbon skeleton and well-developed porous structure. Our previous work shows that embedding graphene quantum dots with high crystallinity and abundant functional groups into CNFs can construct entire reinforcing phase, leading to significantly improved mechanical strength and conductivity[30,33].

Herein, we reported a strong flexible RCNF though introducing small-sized CDs into the CNF (Fig. 6 a). RCNF show good flexibility and malleability, which can recover to its initial shape after cyclic grasping (Fig. 6b). Moreover, 0.6 mg RCNF fabrics (4*23 cm) also act as a rope to pull up a weight with 50 g, revealing the superior mechanical strength (Fig. 6c). The SEM images of the CNF and RCNF are shown in Fig. 6d and e. It should be mentioned that the average fiber diameters increase from 128.3 nm of CNF to 201.2 nm of RCNF with the introduction of CDs. The morphology of the electrospun nanofibers mainly depends on the viscosity of the spinning solution. CDs with abundant functional groups show strong interaction with PAN molecules to increase the viscosity of the spinning solution, leading to an increased fiber diameter. Through chemical activation, it is realized that the activated RCNF balance the contradiction between a strong carbon skeleton and well-developed porous structure. The burn-off ratio after activation is ca. 65.8 wt% (Table S3). The SEM image and average fiber diameter of the activated RCNF are shown in Figure S6. The average fiber diameter of the activated RCNF is 186 nm, smaller than RCNF of 201 nm, due to the etching during chemical activation. However, activated RCNF shows an ultrahigh specific surface area (1921 m2 g−1), much higher than RCNF (16 m2 g−1) and CNF (121 m2 g−1). Moreover, it shows an electric conductivity of 51.8 S m−1, which is slightly lower than RCNF (76.6 S m−1) but 4.2 times higher than CNF (12.2 S m−1, Fig. 6f and g). In addition, mechanical test proves good mechanical properties of RCNF (4.1 MPa) and activated RCNF (2.2 MPa, Fig. 6g and Figure S7).

CDs play a crucial bifunctional role in forming an entire conductive network and reinforcing phase inside the CNF [30,33], ensuring the activated RCNF with a high surface area, good conductivity, and robust mechanical property.

The electrochemical performance of the activated RCNF was measured using a three-electrode system. The rectangular shape of CV curves at different scan rates varying from 10 to 100 mV s−1 and isosceles triangular shape of GCD curves at different current densities from 1 to 100 A g−1 indicate their primary electric double-layer behavior (Fig. 7 a and b). The activated RCNF show the highest capacitance of 320.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 as well as remarkably high capacitance retentions of 75.6% at 100 A g−1, which are much better than the as-prepared CNF (112 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), RCNF (135 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and most of the reported carbon materials (Fig. 7c)[30,70–74]. The relaxation time constant (τ0) of the activated RCNF is only 0.33 s in Fig. 7d, demonstrating outstanding power ability. Therefore, CDs derived from CLR can improve the electrical and mechanical properties of CNF owing to their novel physicochemical properties.

In summary, we have prepared functional CDs from CLR through a mild oxidation. The piranha solution was used to oxidize and cut highly reactive aliphatic chains in CLR into oxygen-bearing functional groups at the edge sites. The obtained CDs have an average diameter of 6.8 nm, conjugated cores, enriched oxygen-bearing functional groups (oxygen content 25 at.%), and high yield of ca. 36%. Benefiting from the novel structure, we construct microporous carbon with both high surface area (1345 m2 g−1) and high density (0.86 g cm−3) through homogeneous activation of CDs. The microporous carbon show high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of 262 F g−1/250 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and good rate performance of 103 F g−1/83 F cm−3 at 100 A g−1, higher than or comparable to most of the reported carbon materials. We embedded CDs into the electrospun CNF to construct entire reinforcing phase and conductive networks. The activated RCNF shows high specific surface area (1921 m2 g−1), high mechanical strength, high flexibility, and high capacitive energy storage performance. Therefore, our work provides a new thought for the high value-added utilization of CLR, which may put forward the development of direct coal liquefaction.

Fuwei Qin: Writing – original draft. Qiqi Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Tingting Tang: Formal analysis. Jiayao Zhu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Xuemeng Gan: Formal analysis. Yaoyao Chen: Formal analysis. Yizhao Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Xueli Huang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Dianzeng Jia: Formal analysis, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046, 21766036, 52100166), the Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Xinjiang, the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2022.124216.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
